summary:
The Merovingian Dynasty (c. 481–751 AD) was the first Frankish royal house — founded by [[event:2041|Clovis I]] and ruling the Frankish kingdoms (eventually united under a single king, then repeatedly divided among heirs) for nearly three centuries. The dynasty's name derives from the legendary ancestor Merovech; its members bore their long hair as a mark of royal status (the 'long-haired kings'). The Merovingian period saw the [[event:2041|Frankish]] conversion to Catholicism, the establishment of Frankish dominance over Gaul, and the creation of the administrative and ecclesiastical structures that [[event:2138|Charlemagne]] would later build upon.

Merovingian rule was characterized by the Frankish inheritance custom of dividing the kingdom among all male heirs, producing constant division and warfare among brothers and cousins. The resulting political instability led to the rise of the 'mayors of the palace' (maiores domus) — household administrators who gradually accumulated the real power while Merovingian kings became ceremonial 'do-nothing kings' (rois fainéants). The most important mayor was Charles Martel, who won the [[event:2132|Battle of Tours]] (732 AD) and effectively ruled the Frankish kingdoms; his son Pepin III finally deposed the last Merovingian, Childeric III, in 751 AD with papal blessing, founding the Carolingian dynasty. The Merovingian period bequeathed important cultural achievements — the 'Treasure of Childeric' (burial goods from the dynasty's founder) are among the most spectacular examples of Migration Period Germanic art — and the administrative divisions of the Frankish kingdom that influenced medieval French geography.
